title: CDISC with R
text:
Welcome
TODO: opening hook, two or three sentences, in the house register (concrete, second person — see the reference course’s “Most statistics courses start with theory. This one starts with your data.”).
This course teaches CDISC clinical data standards by building them: you take raw data from a simulated Phase III trial (codename GLPX-001) and carry it all the way to submission-ready datasets and outputs, in R.

title: CDISC with R
text:
Who This Course Is For
TODO: audience description — statistical programmers, biostatisticians, data managers and researchers moving into clinical trial work in the pharmaceutical industry.






NoteScope and level



TODO: what this course is and deliberately is not.

title: CDISC with R
section: What This Course Expects From You
text:
What This Course Expects From You
TODO: prerequisites (working R, comfort with the tidyverse, no prior CDISC knowledge assumed).

title: CDISC with R
text:
How the Course Is Organised
TODO: one short paragraph per part:

Part 1: Foundations — why standards exist, and the GLPX-001 trial you will work with throughout.
Part 2: SDTM — TODO.
Part 3: ADaM — TODO.
Part 4: Outputs and Submission — TODO.

All data in this course is synthetic, generated by R/simulate_trial.R. No real patient information is used anywhere.

Exercises are included in this book; worked solutions, extra exercises and instructor material are in the paid tier.

title: 1  Before You Begin: Setup
text:
1.1 What You Will Install
TODO: R, an IDE (RStudio or Positron), Quarto, and Git.

title: 1  Before You Begin: Setup
section: 1.2 Why This Course Pins Its Packages
text:
1.2 Why This Course Pins Its Packages
TODO: explain that reproducibility is part of the subject matter in regulated clinical work, and that this repository practises what it teaches: renv.lock records the exact version of every package the course uses, and restoring it is your first taste of a reproducible pipeline. The lockfile is a teaching artifact, not overhead.

title: 1  Before You Begin: Setup
text:
1.3 Restore the Course Library
TODO: walk through cloning the repo and running, once, in R at the project root:
renv::restore()
TODO: show what a successful restore looks like, and note the expected duration (minutes, not tens of minutes — the project’s .Rprofile points Linux machines at precompiled binaries; macOS and Windows get binaries by default).
